strengths.trait: high-volume code implementation from prescriptive specs
strengths.notes: CONFIRMED across 2 projects: (1) R/Shiny migration — 13 phases, ~7000 lines, 9 parallel + 4 sequential. (2) Agent automation framework — 7 phases implementing PowerShell scripts, bash runner, Python parallel runner, JSON schemas, state files, routing intelligence. Codex is now proven across R, Python, Bash, PowerShell, and JSON schema — not just one language/framework. Confidence raised 0.8 → 0.85.
All 10 instances ran pytest (366 passed). All honestly reported 'Rscript not available in this environment' rather than claiming R validation. Consistent self-awareness about verification boundaries.

strengths.trait: goes beyond spec with tasteful improvements
strengths.notes: Phase 0: shutdown_event.wait, _ensure_parent_dir, Dockerfile layer caching. Unit 2: ggplotly hybrid with static fallback. Unit 5: CLT-aware normal overlay (solid FAIL vs dashed WARN). All praised, not flagged as scope violations.

Phase 2 instance detected that app.R had been modified by another instance (Unit 9 wiring present). Escalated to user rather than silently overwriting. Shows situational awareness in parallel execution contexts.

strengths.trait: respects scope constraints even when they conflict with task goals
strengths.notes: Phase 12 prompt said 'delete distributions.py' but Codex found it was imported by viz/unit6-9.py which it was constrained from modifying. Instead of deleting and breaking imports OR silently editing restricted files, Codex flagged the blocker and offered two options. Correct behavior — respects the constraint hierarchy.

weaknesses.notes: Confirmed across all 10 parallel instances: Rscript not available, Docker daemon unavailable. Container/R-dependent validation always left unverified.

weaknesses.trait: API signature drift in parallel execution
weaknesses.notes: Without visibility into other instances, each Codex instance made independent API design choices. Units 1-4 used minimal (id, pane_data), units 5-9 used full (id, pane_data, heartbeat, has_first_tick, active_tab). Root cause: prompt left room for interpretation on module server arguments.

All 10 instances modified app.R. Last writer wins — result was incomplete wiring (only 5 of 9 units connected). This is a fundamental hazard of parallel-on-same-branch execution, not a Codex-specific weakness, but must be designed around.

prompt_requirements.scope_constraints: CC explicitly scopes what Codex may and may NOT touch.

parallelization.reason: each per-phase prompt is self-contained with all patterns embedded inline — Codex doesn't need to see prior unit code
parallelization.merge_strategy: REVISED: per-branch execution + CC merge layer. All-on-master creates last-writer-wins on shared files (app.R).
lessons: Lock exact function signatures in prompts — no interpretation room
Use per-branch execution to prevent shared-file corruption
Track phase assignment to prevent duplicate execution
CC must serve as merge resolver after fan-out

relationship_to_claude_code.notes: Codex executes → CC reviews → CC merges (if parallel) → CC commits/pushes. CC is the quality gate AND the merge layer. Codex never commits directly.

phase_results.notes: Asked question about concurrent app.R changes — showed situational awareness
Scatter+LSRL, residual fix, QQ plot, ggplotly hybrid
Timeline, volume bars, staleness gauge, OHLCV reactable
SE curve, bootstrap histogram, CLT badge, effective n
Normal curve + native tail shading, CI number line, fill-rate, two-prop
t-curve + dt shading, single CI, stacked CI, df>30 annotation
Chi-square curve, obs vs expected, segmented bar, contributions reactable
DUPLICATE — two instances produced output. CI/PI fix, residual x-axis fix, slope t-curve, QQ
CC-reviewed and committed. 1170 lines — largest module. Full HTTP recompute/history/pin flow.
Cutover complete. Deleted dashboard.html, stripped server to pipeline API only, updated tests. Correctly identified distributions.py blocker and respected scope constraint.
